party_a.current_emotional_state: calm_and_focused — Party A has maintained consistent, procedural framing without escalation; concern is articulated clearly and remains child-centered

emotional_arc.assessment: calm_and_clear — Party A articulated a specific procedural concern without evident distress or escalation; tone is constructive
calm_and_focused — Party A has maintained consistent, procedural framing without escalation; concern is articulated clearly and remains child-centered

accumulated_interests.interest: predictability and stability for the child during holiday/break transitions
advance notice to support child's psychological preparation for schedule changes
clarity about when changes can occur (implicit boundary concern)
Child psychological stability and preparation time for transitions
Predictability in schedule planning to reduce child anxiety
Clear boundaries around when/how changes occur (process control)

relational_posture_progression.posture: engaged_and_forward_looking — Party A has entered the process with a concrete concern, suggesting readiness to participate; framing is child-centered rather than adversarial
engaged_and_solution_focused — Party A entered with a concrete concern and has not escalated; framing remains constructive

scaffold_divergence_log.model_value: unobserved — Party A has made an opening statement; Party B has not yet responded. Too early to assess whether this will be collaborative, contested, or asymmetric. Party A's framing is non-blaming, which is a positive process signal, but the dynamic remains latent until Party B engages.

cooperative_with_structural_tension — Both parties are solution-focused and non-adversarial, but they have identified a classic predictability-vs-flexibility tradeoff. Party A prioritizes advance notice and child preparation; Party B prioritizes adaptability with fairness safeguards. This is a structural tension, not a relational conflict. The dynamic is healthy — they are problem-solving a real coordination challenge.

notes: Turn 1 — no party input received yet
Cannot assess emotional state, interests, or relational posture without observation of party behavior
Scaffold indicates apparently_stable states but this is predictive rather than observed
Appropriate stance is structured openness with process framing
Party A's concern is narrow and procedural — this could be a high-priority issue or one item in a longer list; we don't yet know
Party A used child-centered language ('so the child is not surprised') — this may reflect genuine child focus or strategic framing; too early to distinguish
Party B's silence is not yet meaningful — this is only T3 and Party A spoke at T2; Party B has had no invitation to respond
No relational tension observable yet — process is still in opening mode
Both parties have now contributed substantive interests. The tension is structural (competing values) not relational (antagonism).
Party B's phrasing 'flexibility matters too' is a relational bridge — acknowledges Party A's concern while introducing a competing need.

Neither party has shared the experiential or emotional driver behind their stated concern. Party A's predictability focus may be rooted in past disruptions; Party B's flexibility need may be rooted in anticipated work/life demands. This depth has not been invited yet.

The perception agent's note about 'premature convergence' is valid — both parties jumped to mechanisms (notice windows, written confirmation, makeup time) without exploring what drives the need. Risk of solutions that don't address root concerns.
No safety signals. Both parties are psychologically present and relationally stable.
